text: We present a case of a patient that has been for three years with a gastrocutaneous fistula after undergoing a subtotal gastrectomy for a bleeding gastric ulcer. The patient had attempted surgical approaches and has been treated conservatively. for a period of three years. Here you can see the esophageal gastroenteroscopy showing a small fistula extract and an upper GI series showing also the fistula extract into the left upper quadrant.

The surgeon navigates through the abdominal cavity, encountering large amounts of adhesions that complicate access to the intraperitoneal space. Sharp dissection is used to carefully separate adhesions in the upper abdomen, avoiding electrocautery until the absence of whole viscera is confirmed.

The surgeon identifies the diaphragmatic crura on the right side, followed by the esophagus, aorta, and vena cava. The rule limb leading to the gastrojejunostomy is divided, and the left gastric artery is taken down using a white cartridge linear stapler.

The surgeon gains control of the esophagus and divides it using a blue cartridge linear stapler. The Roux limb is recreated, and an esophagotomy is performed. The gastrojejunostomy is resected, followed by reconstruction with an esophagojejunostomy in a side-to-side fashion. A lateral-lateral anastomosis is completed using a blue cartridge linear stapler, and a 32 French UL tube is passed. The defect is closed with a double layer of running theovargal sutures.

The surgical field is obscured by significant bleeding, but the team attempts laparoscopic visualization of the lower mediastinum and diaphragm cruciate structures near the retroperitoneal area. Leak tests are conducted using methylene blue and air via endoscope, and the esophageal jejunostomy is secured.

to the right crux of the diaphragm to prevent that area to herniate into the lower mediastinum. This patient had a postoperative uneventful period. He was resumed on PO diet for four days and discharged home on postoperative day number six. with a drain left in place, tolerating PO diet, and with totally stable vital signs.
